conj.statement: A policy framework for 'Technological Mediation' grounded in phenomenological bracketing of natural attitude, structured as: (1) First-Person Embodiment Protocols requiring lived-experience documentation from technologically mediated practices across all geographic scales; (2) Temporal Finitude Safeguards mandating deliberate exposure windows and discontinuities in algorithmic immersion to preserve existential temporality; (3) Responsibility-Oriented Infrastructure Design principles embedding care ethics through co-constitutive feedback loops between user intentionality and system responsiveness; (4) Power-Structure Transparency Mandates requiring algorithmic opacity audits to expose reductive objectifications of lived worlds; (5) Contextual Bracketing Modules permitting state- and county-level suspension of universal protocols where they conflict with regionally constituted meaning horizons.1. Alaska – extreme geographic isolation necessitates asynchronous, low-bandwidth phenomenological scaffolds; 2. Hawaii – island biogeography and Indigenous cosmologies require decolonial bracketing of settler-technological temporality; 3. New York – hyper-dense urban environments demand spatial-temporal disambiguation protocols to counter embodied overload; 4. Mississippi – historical surveillance legacies necessitate restorative responsibility frameworks prioritizing communal intentionality; 5. California – tech-industry saturation requires counter-movements toward embodied discontinuity and non-digital grounding.1. Across all tiers, technologically mediated experience is consistently bracketed as non-neutrality requiring intentional suspension of natural attitude; 2. All tiers recognize temporality and finitude as non-negotiable phenomenological anchors disrupted by algorithmic acceleration; 3. Power structures manifest identically across tiers as reductive objectifications of lived worlds needing explicit exposure.1. Urban high-fidelity demands conflict with rural low-infrastructure constraints in implementation of embodied scaffolding; 2. Suburban fragmentation protocols diverge from rural communal intentionality models in responsibility framework design; 3. State-level decolonial bracketing (e.g., Hawaii) tensions with federal algorithmic transparency mandates in epistemic authority.
